4.notes:
Exodus - Chapter 4
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God gives Moses three signs to confirm his calling, appoints Aaron as his spokesman, and Moses returns to Egypt.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 4 continues Moses' dialogue with God at the
burning bush. Moses raises four more objections (adding to "Who am I?" from
3:11): "They will not believe me" (v.1), "I am not eloquent" (v.10), and
finally the bare refusal: "Send someone else" (v.13). God answers each
objection with provision — signs, Aaron as spokesman — but His anger burns
at Moses' final resistance. The chapter also contains the mysterious
"bridegroom of blood" incident (vv.24-26), God's declaration of Israel as
His firstborn son (v.22), and the first mention of God hardening Pharaoh's
heart (v.21). Moses finally obeys, meets Aaron, and the elders of Israel
believe and worship.

Moses' Objections and God's Signs (vv.1-9): Objection: "They will not
believe me" (v.1). God gives three signs: (1) The rod becomes a serpent and
back (vv.2-5); (2) Moses' hand becomes leprous then healed (vv.6-7); (3)
Water from the Nile becomes blood (v.9). Each sign demonstrates God's power
over nature, disease, and Egypt's gods.

Moses' Speech Objection (vv.10-12): "I am slow of speech, and of a slow
tongue" (v.10). God's answer: "Who hath made man's mouth?... have not I the
LORD?" (v.11). "I will be with thy mouth" (v.12). The Creator of the mouth
can certainly empower it.

Moses' Final Refusal (vv.13-17): "Send, I pray thee, by the hand of him
whom thou wilt send" — send anyone but me (v.13). God's anger kindles (v.14).
He appoints Aaron as Moses' spokesman (vv.14-16). Moses will be "instead of
God" to Aaron; Aaron will be Moses' "mouth" (v.16). Moses takes "the rod of
God" (v.17).

The Journey to Egypt (vv.18-23): Moses departs Midian (v.18). God tells him
those who sought his life are dead (v.19). God warns: "I will harden his
heart" (v.21). God declares: "Israel is my son, even my firstborn" (v.22) —
and warns Pharaoh: refuse, and "I will slay thy son, even thy firstborn"
(v.23). The tenth plague is announced before the first.

The Bridegroom of Blood (vv.24-26): A mysterious, terrifying incident. God
"sought to kill" Moses (v.24). Zipporah circumcises their son and touches
Moses' feet with the foreskin: "A bloody husband art thou to me" (v.25). God
relents (v.26). Moses had neglected circumcision — the sign of the covenant
he was sent to uphold.

Reunion and Belief (vv.27-31): God sends Aaron to meet Moses (v.27). Moses
tells Aaron everything (v.28). They gather the elders, perform the signs
(vv.29-30). "The people believed... they bowed their heads and worshipped"
(v.31).

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Horeb/Sinai: Moses is still at the mountain of God, continuing the burning bush dialogue from chapter 3.
- Midian (v.18): Moses returns to Jethro before departing for Egypt.
- Egypt (v.19-20): Moses takes his family and journeys back to Egypt, the land he fled 40 years earlier.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.10             | Jeremiah    | 1       | 6                  | I do not know how to speak — for I am only a youth
v.14             | Numbers     | 12      | 2                  | Aaron as spokesman — has the LORD spoken only through Moses?
v.22             | Romans      | 9       | 4                  | Israel my firstborn son — to them belong the adoption
v.24-26          | Genesis     | 17      | 14                 | Circumcision required — any uncircumcised male shall be cut off
v.11             | Psalm       | 94      | 9                  | He who formed the mouth — shall he not speak?
v.31             | Exodus      | 3       | 16                 | The people believed and bowed their heads in worship

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/exodus-4/
  Guzik covers Moses' escalating objections, God's patient provision of
  signs, the significance of "Israel is my firstborn," and the difficult
  "bridegroom of blood" passage.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "What Is That in Thine Hand?" on v.2 (MTP Vol. 9, No. 495)
  "What is that in thine hand? A rod — a shepherd's stick. Nothing special.
  Nothing impressive. But God said 'Cast it on the ground' — and it became
  the rod of God. The rod of miracles. The rod that split the sea. What is
  in YOUR hand? A pen? A hammer? A broom? A Bible? Whatever it is — cast it
  before God. Surrender it to Him. And He will make it the instrument of His
  power. God does not ask for what you don't have. He asks for what you DO
  have — and transforms it."

  Sermon: "Who Made Man's Mouth?" on v.11 (MTP Vol. 31, No. 1860)
  "Who hath made man's mouth? The God who made the instrument can certainly
  play it. Moses says 'I cannot speak' — God says 'I made your mouth.' Your
  inability is no obstacle to omnipotence. Stop looking at your limitations
  and look at your God."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.2              | KJV         | "rod" / ESV: "staff" — Hebrew "matteh" — a staff, a shepherd's rod. The ESV uses "staff"; the KJV uses "rod." Both are valid. It becomes "the rod of God" (v.20) — ordinary tools transformed by divine commission.
v.10             | KJV         | "slow of speech, and of a slow tongue" / ESV: "slow of speech and of tongue" — Hebrew "kevad peh ukhvad lashon" — heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue. Both translations capture the sense. Not necessarily a stutter — possibly a lack of fluency in Egyptian after 40 years, or genuine difficulty in public speaking.
v.13             | KJV         | "by the hand of him whom thou wilt send" / ESV: "by the hand of him whom you will send" — Hebrew "beyad tishlach" — literally "by the hand you will send." Both are identical. A polite refusal disguised as deference: "Send whoever you're going to send — just not me."
v.21             | KJV         | "I will harden his heart" / ESV: "I will harden his heart" — Hebrew "achazzeq et libbo" — I will strengthen/make firm his heart. Both translations identical. The hardening is complex: sometimes God hardens (4:21, 9:12), sometimes Pharaoh hardens himself (8:15, 8:32).
v.22             | KJV         | "my firstborn" / ESV: "my firstborn son" — Hebrew "beni bekhori" — my son, my firstborn. The ESV adds "son" for clarity. A declaration of special relationship, inheritance rights, and protection.
v.24             | KJV         | "sought to kill him" / ESV: "sought to put him to death" — Hebrew "vayevaqesh hamito" — sought to put him to death. The ESV is more literal. Terrifying and mysterious — Moses cannot represent the covenant while neglecting its sign (circumcision).
v.25             | KJV         | "a bloody husband" / ESV: "a bridegroom of blood" — Hebrew "chatan damim" — a bridegroom/husband of blood(s). The ESV's "bridegroom" is more precise than the KJV's "husband" for the Hebrew "chatan." Zipporah's words express horror at the bloody rite.
v.2              | ASV         | "A rod" / NET: "A staff" / WEB: "A rod" — NET consistently uses "staff" where ASV/WEB retain "rod" (Hebrew "matteh"). NET's choice loses the connection to Aaron's "rod" that later buds (Numbers 17).
v.10             | ASV         | "Oh, Lord, I am not eloquent... I am slow of speech, and of a slow tongue" — ASV addresses "Jehovah" but uses "Lord" (Adonai) here, showing Moses switches to the submissive title. WEB uniquely addresses "Yahweh" while also saying "O Lord" — preserving both the divine name context and the respectful address.
v.13             | NET         | "please send anyone else whom you wish to send!" — NET's expansive paraphrase makes Moses' reluctance more explicit; WEB simplifies: "please send someone else"; ASV retains the literal Hebrew idiom "by the hand of him whom thou wilt send."
v.24             | WEB         | "Yahweh met Moses and wanted to kill him" — uniquely interpretive; "wanted to kill" softens the Hebrew "sought to kill" (ASV) into intention rather than action. NET: "sought to kill him" matches ASV's literalness.
v.25             | NET         | "touched it to Moses' feet" — NET interprets the ambiguous "cast it at his feet" (ASV/WEB) as deliberate contact, a significant exegetical choice for this notoriously difficult passage.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Circumcision      | The physical sign of God's covenant with Abraham (Genesis 17). Moses had apparently neglected this for his son — perhaps due to Zipporah's objection. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "What is that in thine hand?" (v.2). God asks for what you already have —
   not what you don't. What has God placed in your hand? Surrender it to Him
   and watch Him transform it.

2. "Who hath made man's mouth?" (v.11). Your limitations are not God's
   limitations. Stop using your weaknesses as excuses. The God who made you
   can empower you for whatever He calls you to do.

3. "Send... by the hand of him whom thou wilt send" (v.13). Moses' final
   objection is simply "I don't want to go." Have you ever said no to God?
   His anger burned — but He still used Moses. God is patient, but
   resistance has consequences (Aaron's involvement created problems later).

4. "Israel is my son, even my firstborn" (v.22). God calls you His child.
   You are not an orphan, not a slave — you are a son/daughter of the Most
   High. Live from that identity.

5. The bridegroom of blood (vv.24-26): Moses couldn't lead God's covenant
   people while neglecting God's covenant sign. Is there an area of
   obedience you're neglecting while trying to serve God in other areas?
   Partial obedience is not obedience.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Exodus Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/exodus/

David Guzik — Exodus 4 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/exodus-4/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

5.ESV:
1. Afterward Moses and Aaron went and said to Pharaoh, “Thus says the LORD, the God of Israel, ‘Let my people go, that they may hold a feast to me in the wilderness.’”
2. But Pharaoh said, “Who is the LORD, that I should obey his voice and let Israel go? I do not know the LORD, and moreover, I will not let Israel go.”
3. Then they said, “The God of the Hebrews has met with us. Please let us go a three days’ journey into the wilderness that we may sacrifice to the LORD our God, lest he fall upon us with pestilence or with the sword.”
4. But the king of Egypt said to them, “Moses and Aaron, why do you take the people away from their work? Get back to your burdens.”
5. And Pharaoh said, “Behold, the people of the land are now many, and you make them rest from their burdens!”
6. The same day Pharaoh commanded the taskmasters of the people and their foremen,
7. “You shall no longer give the people straw to make bricks, as in the past; let them go and gather straw for themselves.
8. But the number of bricks that they made in the past you shall impose on them, you shall by no means reduce it, for they are idle. Therefore they cry, ‘Let us go and offer sacrifice to our God.’
9. Let heavier work be laid on the men that they may labor at it and pay no regard to lying words.”
10. So the taskmasters and the foremen of the people went out and said to the people, “Thus says Pharaoh, ‘I will not give you straw.
11. Go and get your straw yourselves wherever you can find it, but your work will not be reduced in the least.’”
12. So the people were scattered throughout all the land of Egypt to gather stubble for straw.
13. The taskmasters were urgent, saying, “Complete your work, your daily task each day, as when there was straw.”
14. And the foremen of the people of Israel, whom Pharaoh’s taskmasters had set over them, were beaten and were asked, “Why have you not done all your task of making bricks today and yesterday, as in the past?”
15. Then the foremen of the people of Israel came and cried to Pharaoh, “Why do you treat your servants like this?
16. No straw is given to your servants, yet they say to us, ‘Make bricks!’ And behold, your servants are beaten; but the fault is in your own people.”
17. But he said, “You are idle, you are idle; that is why you say, ‘Let us go and sacrifice to the LORD.’
18. Go now and work. No straw will be given you, but you must still deliver the same number of bricks.”
19. The foremen of the people of Israel saw that they were in trouble when they said, “You shall by no means reduce your number of bricks, your daily task each day.”
20. They met Moses and Aaron, who were waiting for them, as they came out from Pharaoh;
21. and they said to them, “The LORD look on you and judge, because you have made us stink in the sight of Pharaoh and his servants, and have put a sword in their hand to kill us.”
22. Then Moses turned to the LORD and said, “O Lord, why have you done evil to this people? Why did you ever send me?
23. For since I came to Pharaoh to speak in your name, he has done evil to this people, and you have not delivered your people at all.”
5.KJV:
1. And afterward Moses and Aaron went in, and told Pharaoh, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Let my people go, that they may hold a feast unto me in the wilderness.

5.NET:
1. Afterward Moses and Aaron went to Pharaoh and said, “This is what the Lord, the God of Israel, has said, ‘Release my people so that they may hold a pilgrim feast to me in the wilderness.’”
2. But Pharaoh said, “Who is the Lord that I should obey him by releasing Israel? I do not know the Lord, and I will not release Israel!”
3. And they said, “The God of the Hebrews has met with us. Let us go a three-day journey into the wilderness so that we may sacrifice to the Lord our God, so that he does not strike us with plague or the sword.”

8. But you must require of them the same quota of bricks that they were making before. Do not reduce it, for they are slackers. That is why they are crying, ‘Let us go sacrifice to our God.’

14. The Israelite foremen whom Pharaoh’s slave masters had set over them were beaten and were asked, “Why did you not complete your requirement for brickmaking as in the past—both yesterday and today?”

18. So now, get back to work! You will not be given straw, but you must still produce your quota of bricks!”
19. The Israelite foremen saw that they were in trouble when they were told, “You must not reduce the daily quota of your bricks.”
20. When they went out from Pharaoh, they encountered Moses and Aaron standing there to meet them,
21. and they said to them, “May the Lord look on you and judge, because you have made us stink in the opinion of Pharaoh and his servants, so that you have given them an excuse to kill us!”
22. Moses returned to the Lord, and said, “Lord, why have you caused trouble for this people? Why did you ever send me?
23. From the time I went to speak to Pharaoh in your name, he has caused trouble for this people, and you have certainly not rescued them!”

21. and they said to them, “May Yahweh look at you, and judge, because you have made us a stench to be abhorred in the eyes of Pharaoh, and in the eyes of his servants, to put a sword in their hand to kill us.”

23. For since I came to Pharaoh to speak in your name, he has brought trouble on this people; and you have not rescued your people at all.”
5.notes:
Exodus - Chapter 5
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Moses and Aaron confront Pharaoh who refuses to release Israel and increases their workload; Israel blames Moses for making things worse.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 5 records Moses and Aaron's first confrontation
with Pharaoh — and its disastrous immediate result. Pharaoh's response to
"Let my people go" is defiant: "Who is the LORD, that I should obey his
voice?" (v.2). He increases Israel's labor — demanding the same quota of
bricks without providing straw. The chapter is a study in how obedience to
God can initially make things worse before they get better. Israel's officers
blame Moses; Moses questions God. This is the dark before the dawn — the
situation must get worse to demonstrate that only God (not diplomacy or human
effort) can deliver.

First Confrontation with Pharaoh (vv.1-5): Moses and Aaron deliver God's
message: "Let my people go, that they may hold a feast unto me in the
wilderness" (v.1). Pharaoh's response: "Who is the LORD, that I should obey
his voice? I know not the LORD, neither will I let Israel go" (v.2). He
accuses Moses of making the people idle (v.5).

Pharaoh's Cruelty Increases (vv.6-14): Pharaoh commands: no more straw for
bricks, but the same production quota (vv.7-8). His reasoning: "they be
idle; therefore they cry" (v.8). The people scatter to gather stubble (v.12).
Taskmasters beat the Israelite officers for failing to meet quotas (v.14).

Israel Blames Moses (vv.15-21): The officers appeal to Pharaoh — rejected
(vv.15-18). They confront Moses and Aaron: "The LORD look upon you, and
judge; because ye have made our savour to be abhorred" (v.21). The very
people who worshipped in 4:31 now curse their deliverer.

Moses Questions God (vv.22-23): Moses returns to God with raw honesty:
"Lord, wherefore hast thou so evil entreated this people? why is it that
thou hast sent me?" (v.22). "Neither hast thou delivered thy people at all"
(v.23). Moses expected immediate deliverance — God has a longer plan.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Egypt — Pharaoh's court: Moses and Aaron confront Pharaoh in his palace, likely in the eastern Delta near Goshen.
- The land of Goshen: Where the Israelite slaves gathered straw for bricks and labored under Egyptian taskmasters.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Romans      | 1       | 21                 | They did not honor him as God — who is the LORD that I should obey?
v.22-23          | Psalm       | 13      | 1-2                | How long O LORD — why have you done evil to this people?
v.1              | Hebrews     | 11      | 24-27              | By faith Moses confronted Pharaoh, not fearing the king's anger
v.9              | Nehemiah    | 4       | 11                 | The enemy's strategy: increase burdens so they cannot work for God
v.21             | Jeremiah    | 20      | 7                  | The servants blame Moses — reproach from those you came to help
v.4              | Acts        | 5       | 28                 | You take the people from their labors — authorities resist God's work

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/exodus-5/
  Guzik emphasizes that obedience to God often makes things worse before
  better, Pharaoh's deliberate ignorance of God, and Moses' honest prayer
  as a model for believers in discouragement.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "Who Is the LORD?" on v.2 (MTP Vol. 19, No. 1098)
  "Who is the LORD? — Pharaoh asks this in defiance. He will receive his
  answer in ten plagues. He will learn who the LORD is — through frogs and
  flies and darkness and death. God does not leave this question unanswered.
  Every man who asks 'Who is the LORD that I should obey Him?' will receive
  an answer — either in grace or in judgment. Better to learn who He is at
  the burning bush than at the Red Sea. Better to bow willingly than to be
  broken."

  Sermon: "Things Getting Worse Before Better" on v.22 (MTP Vol. 24, No. 1420)
  "Neither hast thou delivered thy people AT ALL — Moses expected instant
  results. God had a process. The bricks without straw were necessary — to
  break Israel's attachment to Egypt, to demonstrate that only GOD could
  deliver, to prove that human diplomacy was useless. Christian, when you
  obey God and things get worse — do not conclude that God has failed. He is
  working. The darkest hour precedes the dawn."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.1              | KJV         | "Let my people go" / ESV: "Let my people go" — Hebrew "shallach et ammi" — send away / release my people. Both translations identical. A royal command from the King of kings to an earthly king.
v.2              | KJV         | "I know not the LORD" / ESV: "I do not know the LORD" — Hebrew "lo yadati et YHWH" — I do not acknowledge/recognize YHWH. Both translations equivalent. Not ignorance but defiance — Pharaoh refuses to recognize YHWH's authority.
v.7              | KJV         | "straw" / ESV: "straw" — Hebrew "teben" — chopped straw mixed with clay to strengthen bricks. Without it, bricks crumble. Both translations identical.
v.8              | KJV         | "the tale of the bricks" / ESV: "the number of bricks" — Hebrew "matkonet hallevenim." The KJV's "tale" = tally/count (archaic English). The ESV's "number" is the modern equivalent. Same meaning: the production quota remains unchanged.
v.21             | KJV         | "made our savour to be abhorred" / ESV: "made us stink" — Hebrew "hiv'ashtem et reichenu" — you have made our smell/reputation stink. The ESV is more blunt and literal; the KJV is more formal. Both capture the vivid metaphor: Moses has made Israel repulsive to Pharaoh.
v.22             | KJV         | "evil entreated" / ESV: "done evil to" — Hebrew "hare'ota" — to do evil to, to bring harm upon. The ESV is more direct. Moses accuses God of harming His own people — raw, honest prayer.
v.1              | NET         | "Release my people so that they may hold a pilgrim feast" — NET uniquely adds "pilgrim" to qualify the feast, interpreting the Hebrew "chag" as a pilgrimage festival; ASV/WEB simply say "feast."
v.2              | WEB         | "Who is Yahweh, that I should listen to his voice to let Israel go? I don't know Yahweh" — by spelling out the divine name, WEB makes Pharaoh's defiance more personal and confrontational — he rejects a named deity, not an abstract title.
v.8              | NET         | "they are slackers" — vivid modern idiom for Hebrew "nirpim" (idle/slack); ASV: "they are idle"; WEB: "they are idle." NET's colloquial choice captures Pharaoh's contempt effectively.
v.21             | NET         | "you have made us stink in the opinion of Pharaoh" — captures the Hebrew idiom "hiv'ashtem et reichenu" (made our scent stink) more forcefully than ASV's formal "made our savor to be abhorred." WEB: "made us a stench."
v.23             | NET         | "you have certainly not rescued them!" — the emphatic construction mirrors the Hebrew infinitive absolute "v'hatzel lo hitzalta"; ASV: "neither hast thou delivered thy people at all" — preserves the double negative emphasis differently.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
"Let my people go" | God's demand through Moses. The defining phrase of the exodus. A command from the true King to a false one. "Who is the LORD?" — Pharaoh's defiant question. The ten plagues are God's answer: "I AM the LORD" — demonstrated in power. | KJV term
Bricks without straw | Pharaoh's cruel escalation. Demanding the same output without providing necessary materials. A picture of oppressive systems that demand results without support. | 
"Idle" (v.8)      | Pharaoh's interpretation of worship. The world often views devotion to God as laziness or escapism. | 
Moses' lament (vv.22-23) | Honest complaint to God when obedience produces worse results. Not faithlessness but the raw prayer of a struggling servant. God answers in chapter 6. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Who is the LORD, that I should obey his voice?" (v.2). Pharaoh's
   question is answered by the plagues. Is there an area where YOU are
   asking this question — resisting God's authority? He will answer.

2. Obedience made things worse (vv.6-14). Following God doesn't guarantee
   immediate improvement. Sometimes the situation gets harder before
   deliverance comes. Don't measure God's faithfulness by short-term
   results.

3. "Ye have made our savour to be abhorred" (v.21). The people blamed Moses
   — their deliverer. Have you ever blamed the person God sent to help you?
   Discouragement makes us attack allies.

4. "Lord, wherefore hast thou so evil entreated this people?" (v.22). Moses
   prays honestly. God can handle your frustration, your questions, your
   "why?" Bring it to Him — don't stuff it down or walk away.

5. "Neither hast thou delivered thy people at all" (v.23). Moses expected
   instant deliverance. God had a ten-plague process. Are you frustrated
   because God isn't working on YOUR timetable? Trust His process.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Exodus Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/exodus/

David Guzik — Exodus 5 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/exodus-5/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

6.ESV:
1. But the LORD said to Moses, “Now you shall see what I will do to Pharaoh; for with a strong hand he will send them out, and with a strong hand he will drive them out of his land.”
2. God spoke to Moses and said to him, “I am the LORD.
3. I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, as God Almighty, but by my name the LORD I did not make myself known to them.
4. I also established my covenant with them to give them the land of Canaan, the land in which they lived as sojourners.
5. Moreover, I have heard the groaning of the people of Israel whom the Egyptians hold as slaves, and I have remembered my covenant.
6. Say therefore to the people of Israel, ‘I am the LORD, and I will bring you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians, and I will deliver you from slavery to them, and I will redeem you with an outstretched arm and with great acts of judgment.
7. I will take you to be my people, and I will be your God, and you shall know that I am the LORD your God, who has brought you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians.
8. I will bring you into the land that I swore to give to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob. I will give it to you for a possession. I am the LORD.’”
9. Moses spoke thus to the people of Israel, but they did not listen to Moses, because of their broken spirit and harsh slavery.
10. So the LORD said to Moses,
11. “Go in, tell Pharaoh king of Egypt to let the people of Israel go out of his land.”
12. But Moses said to the LORD, “Behold, the people of Israel have not listened to me. How then shall Pharaoh listen to me, for I am of uncircumcised lips?”
13. But the LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron and gave them a charge about the people of Israel and about Pharaoh king of Egypt: to bring the people of Israel out of the land of Egypt.
14. These are the heads of their fathers’ houses: the sons of Reuben, the firstborn of Israel: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi; these are the clans of Reuben.
15. The sons of Simeon: Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar, and Shaul, the son of a Canaanite woman; these are the clans of Simeon.
16. These are the names of the sons of Levi according to their generations: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari, the years of the life of Levi being 137 years.
17. The sons of Gershon: Libni and Shimei, by their clans.
18. The sons of Kohath: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel, the years of the life of Kohath being 133 years.
19. The sons of Merari: Mahli and Mushi. These are the clans of the Levites according to their generations.
20. Amram took as his wife Jochebed his father’s sister, and she bore him Aaron and Moses, the years of the life of Amram being 137 years.
21. The sons of Izhar: Korah, Nepheg, and Zichri.
22. The sons of Uzziel: Mishael, Elzaphan, and Sithri.
23. Aaron took as his wife Elisheba, the daughter of Amminadab and the sister of Nahshon, and she bore him Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.
24. The sons of Korah: Assir, Elkanah, and Abiasaph; these are the clans of the Korahites.
25. Eleazar, Aaron’s son, took as his wife one of the daughters of Putiel, and she bore him Phinehas. These are the heads of the fathers’ houses of the Levites by their clans.
26. These are the Aaron and Moses to whom the LORD said: “Bring out the people of Israel from the land of Egypt by their hosts.”
27. It was they who spoke to Pharaoh king of Egypt about bringing out the people of Israel from Egypt, this Moses and this Aaron.
28. On the day when the LORD spoke to Moses in the land of Egypt,
29. the LORD said to Moses, “I am the LORD; tell Pharaoh king of Egypt all that I say to you.”
30. But Moses said to the LORD, “Behold, I am of uncircumcised lips. How will Pharaoh listen to me?”

3. And I appeared unto Abraham, unto Isaac, and unto Jacob, by the name of God Almighty, but by my name JEHOVAH was I not known to them.

14. These be the heads of their fathers’ houses: The sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel; Hanoch, and Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi: these be the families of Reuben.

18. And the sons of Kohath; Amram, and Izhar, and Hebron, and Uzziel: and the years of the life of Kohath were an hundred thirty and three years.
19. And the sons of Merari; Mahali and Mushi: these are the families of Levi according to their generations.
20. And Amram took him Jochebed his father’s sister to wife; and she bare him Aaron and Moses: and the years of the life of Amram were an hundred and thirty and seven years.
21. And the sons of Izhar; Korah, and Nepheg, and Zichri.
22. And the sons of Uzziel; Mishael, and Elzaphan, and Zithri.
23. And Aaron took him Elisheba, daughter of Amminadab, sister of Naashon, to wife; and she bare him Nadab, and Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.

6.notes:
Exodus - Chapter 6
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God reaffirms His covenant promises to Moses and commands him to return to Pharaoh; the genealogy of Moses and Aaron is recorded.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 6 is God's answer to Moses' complaint in 5:22-23.
God responds not with rebuke but with revelation — a deeper unveiling of His
name, His covenant, and His plan. The "I AM the LORD" declaration (v.2) is
followed by seven "I will" promises (vv.6-8) — God's comprehensive plan of
redemption. Yet Israel cannot hear it "for anguish of spirit, and for cruel
bondage" (v.9) — suffering can deafen us to God's promises. The genealogy
(vv.14-25) establishes Moses and Aaron's credentials within the tribe of
Levi. The chapter ends where it began: Moses' objection about his
"uncircumcised lips" (v.12, 30).

God's Self-Revelation (vv.1-8): God answers Moses' complaint: "Now shalt
thou see what I will do" (v.1). He reveals Himself: "I am the LORD (YHWH)"
(v.2). He appeared to the patriarchs as "God Almighty" (El Shaddai) but now
reveals the fuller meaning of YHWH (v.3). He has heard Israel's groaning and
remembered His covenant (v.5). Then the seven "I will" promises:
  1. "I will bring you out" (v.6)
  2. "I will rid you out of their bondage" (v.6)
  3. "I will redeem you" (v.6)
  4. "I will take you to me for a people" (v.7)
  5. "I will be to you a God" (v.7)
  6. "I will bring you in unto the land" (v.8)
  7. "I will give it you for an heritage" (v.8)

Israel's Deafness (v.9): Moses delivers God's message, "but they hearkened
not unto Moses for anguish of spirit, and for cruel bondage." Suffering had
made them unable to receive hope.

Moses' Renewed Objection (vv.10-13): God commands Moses to go to Pharaoh
again (v.11). Moses objects: "The children of Israel have not hearkened unto
me; how then shall Pharaoh hear me, who am of uncircumcised lips?" (v.12).

The Genealogy (vv.14-27): A selective genealogy tracing Reuben, Simeon, and
especially Levi — establishing Moses and Aaron's lineage. Key names: Amram
and Jochebed (Moses' parents, v.20), Aaron's wife Elisheba (v.23), Aaron's
sons including Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar (v.23), and Phinehas
(v.25). The genealogy authenticates Moses and Aaron as God's chosen
instruments.

Renewed Commission (vv.28-30): The narrative resumes: God commands Moses to
speak to Pharaoh (v.29). Moses repeats his objection: "I am of uncircumcised
lips" (v.30) — setting up God's answer in chapter 7.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Egypt: Moses and Aaron operate in Pharaoh's court and among the Israelites in Goshen.
- The genealogy (vv.14-25) traces the family back to Israel's entry into Egypt, connecting this generation to the land of promise given to Abraham.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.6-7            | Titus       | 2       | 14                 | Gave himself to redeem us from all lawlessness
v.7              | Jeremiah    | 31      | 33                 | I will be their God and they shall be my people
v.7              | Revelation  | 21      | 3                  | God himself will be with them as their God
v.6              | Galatians   | 4       | 4-5                | God sent forth his Son to redeem those under the law
v.3              | Genesis     | 17      | 1                  | I am God Almighty — El Shaddai revealed to Abraham
v.9              | Isaiah      | 61      | 1                  | Broken spirit prevents hearing — bind up the brokenhearted

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/exodus-6/
  Guzik emphasizes the seven "I will" promises as a complete picture of
  redemption, the progressive revelation of God's name, and the tragedy of
  Israel's inability to hear due to suffering.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "The Seven I Wills" on vv.6-8 (MTP Vol. 28, No. 1672)
  "Seven times God says 'I WILL' — and every 'I will' of God is as certain
  as if it were already done. I will bring you out — deliverance. I will rid
  you — freedom. I will redeem you — purchase. I will take you — relationship.
  I will be your God — identity. I will bring you in — inheritance. I will
  give it you — possession. From bondage to inheritance in seven divine
  declarations. And not one 'you must' among them. This is grace: God does
  it all. Our part? Believe it. Receive it. Rest in it."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.3              | KJV         | "by my name JEHOVAH was I not known to them" / ESV: "by my name the LORD I did not make myself known to them" — Hebrew "ushmi YHWH lo nodati lahem." The ESV's "make myself known" captures the reflexive Hebrew verb better. This does not mean the patriarchs never heard the name YHWH (Genesis 15:7 uses it), but that they did not EXPERIENCE its full meaning — God as covenant-keeper who acts in history to redeem.
v.6              | KJV         | "redeem" / ESV: "redeem" — Hebrew "ga'al" — to redeem as a kinsman-redeemer. Both translations identical. God acts as Israel's go'el — their nearest relative who pays the price to set them free.
v.6              | KJV         | "stretched out arm" / ESV: "outstretched arm" — Hebrew "zeroa netuyah" — an extended, powerful arm. Both translations equivalent. Military imagery: God as warrior fighting for His people.
v.9              | KJV         | "anguish of spirit" / ESV: "broken spirit" — Hebrew "qotser ruach" — shortness of spirit/breath. The KJV's "anguish" and the ESV's "broken spirit" both capture the idea, though the Hebrew is literally "shortness of breath" — spiritual suffocation from oppression.
v.12             | KJV         | "uncircumcised lips" / ESV: "uncircumcised lips" — Hebrew "aral sefatayim" — lips that are blocked, closed, unfit. Both translations identical. A metaphor: as uncircumcised flesh is unfit for covenant, so Moses' lips feel unfit for God's message.
v.3              | ASV         | "by my name Jehovah I was not known to them" / WEB: "by my name Yahweh I was not known to them" — both make God's personal name explicit where ESV/KJV hide it behind "the LORD." This verse's theology is much clearer when the actual name is visible.
v.9              | NET         | "because of their discouragement and hard labor" — interpretive paraphrase of Hebrew "miqotser ruach" (shortness of spirit); ASV/WEB retain "anguish of spirit" — more literal and emotionally vivid. - v.12 — ASV/WEB: "uncircumcised lips" — preserves the striking Hebrew metaphor "aral sefatayim"; NET paraphrases to "I speak with difficulty," losing the theological resonance of "uncircumcised" as a barrier metaphor.
v.26             | ASV         | "according to their hosts" / WEB: "according to their armies" / NET: "by their regiments" — three different renderings of Hebrew "tsiv'otam." NET's "regiments" is uniquely military; WEB's "armies" is similar; ASV's "hosts" preserves the word used in "LORD of hosts."
v.8              | NET         | "I will give it to you as a possession" — renders Hebrew "morasha" (heritage/inheritance) as "possession," a more legal/concrete term; ASV/WEB retain "heritage," which carries covenantal weight.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
El Shaddai        | "God Almighty" — the name by which God related to the patriarchs. Emphasizes power and provision. YHWH adds covenant faithfulness and redemptive action. Seven "I wills" — God's comprehensive redemption plan: deliverance, freedom, redemption, relationship, identity, inheritance, possession. All initiated by God, not earned by Israel. Go'el (Redeemer) — Kinsman-redeemer. God acts as Israel's nearest relative, paying the price for their freedom. Fulfilled ultimately in Christ. "Anguish of spirit" — Literally "shortness of breath." Suffering so intense it prevents receiving hope. Spiritual suffocation. "Uncircumcised lips" — Moses' metaphor for feeling unfit to speak for God. Inadequacy language — answered by God's empowerment. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. The seven "I wills" (vv.6-8): God's redemption is comprehensive. He
   doesn't just free you FROM something — He brings you INTO relationship,
   identity, and inheritance. Which of these seven promises do you need to
   hear today?

2. "They hearkened not... for anguish of spirit" (v.9). Suffering can make
   us deaf to God's promises. If you're in deep pain right now, you may not
   be able to "hear" hope. That's okay. God's promises remain true whether
   you can feel them or not.

3. "I am the LORD" — repeated three times (vv.2, 6, 8). God's identity is
   the foundation of His promises. He can do what He says because He IS who
   He says. Do you know Him — not just His name but His character?

4. Moses objects again (v.12) — even after God's magnificent promises. We
   can hear God's word and still feel inadequate. God doesn't rebuke Moses —
   He provides Aaron. God meets us in our weakness.

5. The genealogy (vv.14-25) grounds the story in real history — real
   families, real people. God works through ordinary lineages. Your family
   history, however messy, is part of God's story.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Exodus Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/exodus/

David Guzik — Exodus 6 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/exodus-6/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

7.ESV:
1. And the LORD said to Moses, “See, I have made you like God to Pharaoh, and your brother Aaron shall be your prophet.
2. You shall speak all that I command you, and your brother Aaron shall tell Pharaoh to let the people of Israel go out of his land.
3. But I will harden Pharaoh’s heart, and though I multiply my signs and wonders in the land of Egypt,
4. Pharaoh will not listen to you. Then I will lay my hand on Egypt and bring my hosts, my people the children of Israel, out of the land of Egypt by great acts of judgment.
5. The Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD, when I stretch out my hand against Egypt and bring out the people of Israel from among them.”
6. Moses and Aaron did so; they did just as the LORD commanded them.
7. Now Moses was eighty years old, and Aaron eighty-three years old, when they spoke to Pharaoh.
8. Then the LORD said to Moses and Aaron,
9. “When Pharaoh says to you, ‘Prove yourselves by working a miracle,’ then you shall say to Aaron, ‘Take your staff and cast it down before Pharaoh, that it may become a serpent.’”
10. So Moses and Aaron went to Pharaoh and did just as the LORD commanded. Aaron cast down his staff before Pharaoh and his servants, and it became a serpent.
11. Then Pharaoh summoned the wise men and the sorcerers, and they, the magicians of Egypt, also did the same by their secret arts.
12. For each man cast down his staff, and they became serpents. But Aaron’s staff swallowed up their staffs.
13. Still Pharaoh’s heart was hardened, and he would not listen to them, as the LORD had said.
14. Then the LORD said to Moses, “Pharaoh’s heart is hardened; he refuses to let the people go.
15. Go to Pharaoh in the morning, as he is going out to the water, and stand on the bank of the Nile to meet him. Take in your hand the staff that turned into a serpent.
16. And you shall say to him, ‘The LORD, the God of the Hebrews, sent me to you, saying, “Let my people go, that they may serve me in the wilderness.” But so far, you have not obeyed.
17. Thus says the LORD, “By this you shall know that I am the LORD: behold, with the staff that is in my hand I will strike the water that is in the Nile, and it shall turn into blood.
18. The fish in the Nile shall die, and the Nile will stink, and the Egyptians will grow weary of drinking water from the Nile.”’”
19. And the LORD said to Moses, “Say to Aaron, ‘Take your staff and stretch out your hand over the waters of Egypt, over their rivers, their canals, and their ponds, and all their pools of water, so that they may become blood, and there shall be blood throughout all the land of Egypt, even in vessels of wood and in vessels of stone.’”
20. Moses and Aaron did as the LORD commanded. In the sight of Pharaoh and in the sight of his servants he lifted up the staff and struck the water in the Nile, and all the water in the Nile turned into blood.
21. And the fish in the Nile died, and the Nile stank, so that the Egyptians could not drink water from the Nile. There was blood throughout all the land of Egypt.
22. But the magicians of Egypt did the same by their secret arts. So Pharaoh’s heart remained hardened, and he would not listen to them, as the LORD had said.
23. Pharaoh turned and went into his house, and he did not take even this to heart.
24. And all the Egyptians dug along the Nile for water to drink, for they could not drink the water of the Nile.
25. Seven full days passed after the LORD had struck the Nile.
7.KJV:
1. And the LORD said unto Moses, See, I have made thee a god to Pharaoh: and Aaron thy brother shall be thy prophet.
2. Thou shalt speak all that I command thee: and Aaron thy brother shall speak unto Pharaoh, that he send the children of Israel out of his land.
3. And I will harden Pharaoh’s heart, and multiply my signs and my wonders in the land of Egypt.
4. But Pharaoh shall not hearken unto you, that I may lay my hand upon Egypt, and bring forth mine armies, and my people the children of Israel, out of the land of Egypt by great judgments.
5. And the Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD, when I stretch forth mine hand upon Egypt, and bring out the children of Israel from among them.
6. And Moses and Aaron did as the LORD commanded them, so did they.
7. And Moses was fourscore years old, and Aaron fourscore and three years old, when they spake unto Pharaoh.
8. And the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Aaron, saying,
9. When Pharaoh shall speak unto you, saying, Shew a miracle for you: then thou shalt say unto Aaron, Take thy rod, and cast it before Pharaoh, and it shall become a serpent.
10. And Moses and Aaron went in unto Pharaoh, and they did so as the LORD had commanded: and Aaron cast down his rod before Pharaoh, and before his servants, and it became a serpent.
11. Then Pharaoh also called the wise men and the sorcerers: now the magicians of Egypt, they also did in like manner with their enchantments.
12. For they cast down every man his rod, and they became serpents: but Aaron’s rod swallowed up their rods.
13. And he hardened Pharaoh’s heart, that he hearkened not unto them; as the LORD had said.
14. And the LORD said unto Moses, Pharaoh’s heart is hardened, he refuseth to let the people go.
15. Get thee unto Pharaoh in the morning; lo, he goeth out unto the water; and thou shalt stand by the river’s brink against he come; and the rod which was turned to a serpent shalt thou take in thine hand.
16. And thou shalt say unto him, The LORD God of the Hebrews hath sent me unto thee, saying, Let my people go, that they may serve me in the wilderness: and, behold, hitherto thou wouldest not hear.
17. Thus saith the LORD, In this thou shalt know that I am the LORD: behold, I will smite with the rod that is in mine hand upon the waters which are in the river, and they shall be turned to blood.
18. And the fish that is in the river shall die, and the river shall stink; and the Egyptians shall lothe to drink of the water of the river.

7.notes:
Exodus - Chapter 7
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Moses and Aaron confront Pharaoh; Aaron's rod becomes a serpent and swallows the magicians' rods, then the first plague turns the Nile to blood.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 7 begins the plague narrative — God's systematic
judgment on Egypt and its gods. God establishes the relationship between
Moses, Aaron, and Pharaoh: Moses is "a god to Pharaoh" and Aaron is his
"prophet" (v.1). The chapter records the sign of Aaron's rod becoming a
serpent (swallowing the magicians' serpents) and the first plague: water
turned to blood. Each plague targets a specific Egyptian deity — the Nile
(Hapi, the Nile god) is struck first. The magicians can imitate but not
reverse the plague. Pharaoh's heart hardens. The battle between YHWH and
Egypt's gods has begun.

God's Commission Restated (vv.1-7): God makes Moses "a god to Pharaoh" —
His authoritative representative (v.1). Aaron is Moses' "prophet" —
spokesman (v.1). God forewarns: "I will harden Pharaoh's heart" (v.3) so
that He may "multiply my signs and my wonders" (v.3). Purpose: "The
Egyptians shall KNOW that I am the LORD" (v.5). Moses is 80, Aaron 83 (v.7).

The Sign of the Serpent (vv.8-13): Pharaoh demands a miracle (v.9). Aaron
casts his rod — it becomes a serpent (v.10). Pharaoh's magicians (Jannes and
Jambres — 2 Timothy 3:8) replicate it (v.11). But Aaron's rod SWALLOWS
theirs (v.12) — God's power consumes counterfeit power. Pharaoh's heart
hardens (v.13).

The First Plague: Water to Blood (vv.14-25): God sends Moses to meet Pharaoh
at the Nile in the morning (v.15). The demand: "Let my people go" (v.16).
The warning: "In this thou shalt know that I am the LORD" (v.17). Aaron
strikes the Nile — all water becomes blood (vv.19-20). Fish die, the river
stinks, Egyptians cannot drink (v.21). The magicians replicate it (v.22) —
they can make the problem worse but cannot fix it. Pharaoh is unmoved (v.23).
The plague lasts seven days (v.25).

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Egypt — Pharaoh's court: The confrontation between Moses/Aaron and Pharaoh continues.
- The Nile River (v.17): Site of the first plague — water turned to blood. The Nile was worshipped as a god (Hapi); God strikes at the heart of Egypt's religion and economy.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.3              | Romans      | 9       | 17-18              | He hardens whom he wills — God raised Pharaoh for this purpose
v.11             | 2 Timothy   | 3       | 8                  | Jannes and Jambres opposed Moses — so these oppose the truth
v.17             | Revelation  | 16      | 4                  | Water turned to blood — the third angel poured his bowl on the rivers
v.5              | Acts        | 7       | 36                 | Moses performed wonders and signs in Egypt
v.13             | Isaiah      | 46      | 10                 | My counsel shall stand — God's purpose will prevail over Pharaoh
v.22             | Psalm       | 95      | 8                  | Do not harden your hearts as at Meribah

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/exodus-7/
  Guzik covers the plague as judgment on Hapi (the Nile god), the
  significance of Aaron's rod swallowing the magicians' rods, and the
  progressive hardening of Pharaoh's heart.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "Aaron's Rod Swallowing the Rods" on v.12 (MTP Vol. 21, No. 1239)
  "Aaron's rod SWALLOWED UP their rods — it did not merely match them or
  outperform them. It consumed them. God's truth does not merely compete
  with error — it devours it. The gospel does not stand alongside false
  religion as an equal option — it swallows it whole. Let the magicians
  produce their best tricks — God's power will consume them all. And note:
  the magicians could IMITATE but never REVERSE. They could turn water to
  blood but could not turn blood back to water. Satan can make problems
  worse; only God can solve them."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.1              | KJV         | "a god to Pharaoh" / ESV: "like God to Pharaoh" — Hebrew "elohim le-Pharaoh." The ESV adds "like" to soften the statement; the KJV is more direct. Moses stands before Pharaoh with God's full backing — as God's authoritative representative.
v.3              | KJV         | "harden" / ESV: "harden" — Hebrew "aqsheh" — to make hard, to stiffen. Both translations identical. In the plague narrative, three different Hebrew words are used: "chazaq" (strengthen), "kaved" (make heavy), "qashah" (make hard).
v.9              | KJV         | "serpent" / ESV: "serpent" — Hebrew "tannin" — a large serpent, dragon, or sea monster. Different from "nachash" (serpent) in 4:3. Both translations use "serpent." "Tannin" may connect to Egyptian dragon/crocodile imagery — Pharaoh's own symbol turned against him.
v.11             | KJV         | "enchantments" / ESV: "secret arts" — Hebrew "lahatim" — secret arts, flames (from "lahat" — to blaze). The ESV's "secret arts" is more accessible than the KJV's "enchantments." Possibly sleight of hand, demonic power, or both.
v.17             | KJV         | "turned to blood" / ESV: "turned into blood" — Hebrew "nehepku ledam" — transformed into blood. Both translations equivalent. Whether literal blood or a blood-like appearance is debated — but the text presents it as supernatural, and the fish dying confirms it was lethal.
v.22             | KJV         | "did so with their enchantments" / ESV: "did the same by their secret arts" — The magicians replicate the plague on a small scale but cannot reverse it. They add to the problem rather than solving it. The ESV's "secret arts" is consistent with v.11.
v.1              | NET         | "I have made you like God to Pharaoh" — adds "like," softening the statement; ASV/WEB: "I have made thee as God to Pharaoh" — preserves the bolder Hebrew construction where Moses IS God's representative, not merely similar to one.
v.4              | NET         | "my regiments, my people the Israelites" — unique military term; ASV: "my hosts"; WEB: "my armies" — all rendering Hebrew "tsiv'ot." NET's "regiments" suggests organized military units.
v.9              | NET         | "it will become a snake" / ASV: "that it become a serpent" / WEB: "that it become a serpent" — NET uses "snake" (common term), while ASV/WEB retain "serpent" (Hebrew "tannin," which can mean sea monster/dragon — not the same word as ch. 4's "nachash").
v.11             | NET         | "by their secret arts" — interprets Hebrew "b'lahateihem" (by their enchantments/flames); ASV: "with their enchantments"; WEB: "with their enchantments." NET's "secret arts" emphasizes the hiddenness of the magic. - v.14 — ASV/WEB: "Pharaoh's heart is stubborn" / NET: "Pharaoh's heart is hard" — Hebrew "kaved" means heavy/weighty. ASV/WEB's "stubborn" interprets the weight as resistance; NET's "hard" matches ESV's rendering.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
The Plagues       | Ten judgments on Egypt, each targeting an Egyptian deity. Purpose: to demonstrate YHWH's supremacy, answer "Who is the LORD?" (5:2), and compel Israel's release. | 
Hapi              | Egyptian god of the Nile and its annual flooding. The first plague strikes Egypt's most sacred river — their source of life becomes death. Jannes and Jambres — Traditional names of Pharaoh's magicians (2 Timothy 3:8). They can imitate God's signs but cannot reverse them — counterfeit power has limits. "Tannin" (serpent/dragon) — A large reptilian creature. Possibly connected to Pharaoh's own royal symbol — the uraeus serpent/cobra on his crown. God turns Pharaoh's symbol against him. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "The Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD" (v.5). God's judgments have
   a revelatory purpose — they reveal who He is. Even in difficult
   circumstances, God is making Himself known. Are you paying attention?

2. "Aaron's rod swallowed up their rods" (v.12). God's power doesn't merely
   compete with counterfeit power — it consumes it. Whatever false thing
   you're trusting in, God's truth will ultimately swallow it. Trust the
   real over the imitation.

3. The magicians could imitate but not reverse (v.22). Satan can make
   problems worse but cannot solve them. Only God can turn blood back to
   water, death back to life, bondage back to freedom. Where are you looking
   for solutions — to counterfeits or to God?

4. "Pharaoh turned and went into his house, neither did he set his heart to
   this" (v.23). Pharaoh saw the miracle and walked away unmoved. You can
   witness God's power and still refuse to respond. Don't harden your heart
   to what God is showing you.

5. Moses is 80 years old (v.7). God's greatest work through Moses begins at

6. It's never too late for God to use you. Your age, your past failures,
   your "uncircumcised lips" — none of it disqualifies you when God
   commissions you.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Exodus Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/exodus/

David Guzik — Exodus 7 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/exodus-7/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

8.ESV:
1. Then the LORD said to Moses, “Go in to Pharaoh and say to him, ‘Thus says the LORD, “Let my people go, that they may serve me.
2. But if you refuse to let them go, behold, I will plague all your country with frogs.
3. The Nile shall swarm with frogs that shall come up into your house and into your bedroom and on your bed and into the houses of your servants and your people, and into your ovens and your kneading bowls.
4. The frogs shall come up on you and on your people and on all your servants.”’”
5. And the LORD said to Moses, “Say to Aaron, ‘Stretch out your hand with your staff over the rivers, over the canals and over the pools, and make frogs come up on the land of Egypt!’”
6. So Aaron stretched out his hand over the waters of Egypt, and the frogs came up and covered the land of Egypt.
7. But the magicians did the same by their secret arts and made frogs come up on the land of Egypt.
8. Then Pharaoh called Moses and Aaron and said, “Plead with the LORD to take away the frogs from me and from my people, and I will let the people go to sacrifice to the LORD.”
9. Moses said to Pharaoh, “Be pleased to command me when I am to plead for you and for your servants and for your people, that the frogs be cut off from you and your houses and be left only in the Nile.”
10. And he said, “Tomorrow.” Moses said, “Be it as you say, so that you may know that there is no one like the LORD our God.
11. The frogs shall go away from you and your houses and your servants and your people. They shall be left only in the Nile.”
12. So Moses and Aaron went out from Pharaoh, and Moses cried to the LORD about the frogs, as he had agreed with Pharaoh.
13. And the LORD did according to the word of Moses. The frogs died out in the houses, the courtyards, and the fields.
14. And they gathered them together in heaps, and the land stank.
15. But when Pharaoh saw that there was a respite, he hardened his heart and would not listen to them, as the LORD had said.
16. Then the LORD said to Moses, “Say to Aaron, ‘Stretch out your staff and strike the dust of the earth, so that it may become gnats in all the land of Egypt.’”
17. And they did so. Aaron stretched out his hand with his staff and struck the dust of the earth, and there were gnats on man and beast. All the dust of the earth became gnats in all the land of Egypt.
18. The magicians tried by their secret arts to produce gnats, but they could not. So there were gnats on man and beast.
19. Then the magicians said to Pharaoh, “This is the finger of God.” But Pharaoh’s heart was hardened, and he would not listen to them, as the LORD had said.
20. Then the LORD said to Moses, “Rise up early in the morning and present yourself to Pharaoh, as he goes out to the water, and say to him, ‘Thus says the LORD, “Let my people go, that they may serve me.
21. Or else, if you will not let my people go, behold, I will send swarms of flies on you and your servants and your people, and into your houses. And the houses of the Egyptians shall be filled with swarms of flies, and also the ground on which they stand.
22. But on that day I will set apart the land of Goshen, where my people dwell, so that no swarms of flies shall be there, that you may know that I am the LORD in the midst of the earth.
23. Thus I will put a division between my people and your people. Tomorrow this sign shall happen.”’”
24. And the LORD did so. There came great swarms of flies into the house of Pharaoh and into his servants’ houses. Throughout all the land of Egypt the land was ruined by the swarms of flies.
25. Then Pharaoh called Moses and Aaron and said, “Go, sacrifice to your God within the land.”
26. But Moses said, “It would not be right to do so, for the offerings we shall sacrifice to the LORD our God are an abomination to the Egyptians. If we sacrifice offerings abominable to the Egyptians before their eyes, will they not stone us?
27. We must go three days’ journey into the wilderness and sacrifice to the LORD our God as he tells us.”
28. So Pharaoh said, “I will let you go to sacrifice to the LORD your God in the wilderness; only you must not go very far away. Plead for me.”
29. Then Moses said, “Behold, I am going out from you and I will plead with the LORD that the swarms of flies may depart from Pharaoh, from his servants, and from his people, tomorrow. Only let not Pharaoh cheat again by not letting the people go to sacrifice to the LORD.”
30. So Moses went out from Pharaoh and prayed to the LORD.
31. And the LORD did as Moses asked, and removed the swarms of flies from Pharaoh, from his servants, and from his people; not one remained.
32. But Pharaoh hardened his heart this time also, and did not let the people go.
8.KJV:
1. And the LORD spake unto Moses, Go unto Pharaoh, and say unto him, Thus saith the LORD, Let my people go, that they may serve me.
2. And if thou refuse to let them go, behold, I will smite all thy borders with frogs:
3. And the river shall bring forth frogs abundantly, which shall go up and come into thine house, and into thy bedchamber, and upon thy bed, and into the house of thy servants, and upon thy people, and into thine ovens, and into thy kneadingtroughs:
4. And the frogs shall come up both on thee, and upon thy people, and upon all thy servants.
5. And the LORD spake unto Moses, Say unto Aaron, Stretch forth thine hand with thy rod over the streams, over the rivers, and over the ponds, and cause frogs to come up upon the land of Egypt.
6. And Aaron stretched out his hand over the waters of Egypt; and the frogs came up, and covered the land of Egypt.
7. And the magicians did so with their enchantments, and brought up frogs upon the land of Egypt.
8. Then Pharaoh called for Moses and Aaron, and said, Intreat the LORD, that he may take away the frogs from me, and from my people; and I will let the people go, that they may do sacrifice unto the LORD.
9. And Moses said unto Pharaoh, Glory over me: when shall I intreat for thee, and for thy servants, and for thy people, to destroy the frogs from thee and thy houses, that they may remain in the river only?
10. And he said, To morrow. And he said, Be it according to thy word: that thou mayest know that there is none like unto the LORD our God.
11. And the frogs shall depart from thee, and from thy houses, and from thy servants, and from thy people; they shall remain in the river only.
12. And Moses and Aaron went out from Pharaoh: and Moses cried unto the LORD because of the frogs which he had brought against Pharaoh.
13. And the LORD did according to the word of Moses; and the frogs died out of the houses, out of the villages, and out of the fields.
14. And they gathered them together upon heaps: and the land stank.
15. But when Pharaoh saw that there was respite, he hardened his heart, and hearkened not unto them; as the LORD had said.
16. And the LORD said unto Moses, Say unto Aaron, Stretch out thy rod, and smite the dust of the land, that it may become lice throughout all the land of Egypt.
17. And they did so; for Aaron stretched out his hand with his rod, and smote the dust of the earth, and it became lice in man, and in beast; all the dust of the land became lice throughout all the land of Egypt.
18. And the magicians did so with their enchantments to bring forth lice, but they could not: so there were lice upon man, and upon beast.
19. Then the magicians said unto Pharaoh, This is the finger of God: and Pharaoh’s heart was hardened, and he hearkened not unto them; as the LORD had said.
20. And the LORD said unto Moses, Rise up early in the morning, and stand before Pharaoh; lo, he cometh forth to the water; and say unto him, Thus saith the LORD, Let my people go, that they may serve me.
21. Else, if thou wilt not let my people go, behold, I will send swarms of flies upon thee, and upon thy servants, and upon thy people, and into thy houses: and the houses of the Egyptians shall be full of swarms of flies, and also the ground whereon they are.
22. And I will sever in that day the land of Goshen, in which my people dwell, that no swarms of flies shall be there; to the end thou mayest know that I am the LORD in the midst of the earth.
23. And I will put a division between my people and thy people: to morrow shall this sign be.
24. And the LORD did so; and there came a grievous swarm of flies into the house of Pharaoh, and into his servants’ houses, and into all the land of Egypt: the land was corrupted by reason of the swarm of flies.
25. And Pharaoh called for Moses and for Aaron, and said, Go ye, sacrifice to your God in the land.
26. And Moses said, It is not meet so to do; for we shall sacrifice the abomination of the Egyptians to the LORD our God: lo, shall we sacrifice the abomination of the Egyptians before their eyes, and will they not stone us?
